text: This is perhaps the best interpretation of a difficult phrase, ‘With all his glory he received Joppe to be a harbour’. For the language used, cf. 10.58, II Mac. 5.20. Joppe had been captured by Jonathan (10.76), and remained at least nominally in Jewish possession (12.33; 13.11); Simon’s achievement, according to verse 34 below, was to fortify it.
The implication appears to be that both Rome and Sparta sent answers in the manner described.

v. 24. It would be natural to assume that the end of this verse refers to the Roman people; and the Clementine Vulgate, without any manuscript authority, actually inserts the word ‘Roman’. But the proceedings of verses 25 sqq. are evidently those of the Jewish people. There is a want of sequence in the narrative as it stands, and it seems possible that verses 15-24 of chapter 15 originally stood here, and were accidentally misplaced; if so, ‘all this’ will refer to the Roman dispatches.
This name is not found elsewhere, and probably conceals some error in the text.
The Machabaean high priesthood seems to have been regarded as an emergency dispensation. For the mention of the prophet, cf. 4.46.
vv. 45-49. We should perhaps take these verses as part of the decree itself; otherwise verse 48 seems a needless repetition of verse 26.
